source: Public Rust quality talk: Towards Impeccable Rust, Rust Nation UK 2024.
rule_summary: `It works` is not the same as `it is not broken`. Treat passing tests as evidence, not proof, and keep looking for conditions the current evidence does not cover.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: Do not call Rust code correct merely because the happy path or current test suite passes. State what evidence exists and what classes of failure remain untested.
anti_pattern: // cargo test passed, so this parser is correct for all inputs.
good_pattern:
// Evidence: unit tests cover valid/invalid fixtures; property tests cover round-trips.
// Residual risk: malformed Unicode paths are not generated yet.

source: Public Compose podcast discussion, August 2024.
rule_summary: Software quality is never finished. Treat documentation, tests, benchmarks, dependency review, and maintenance as recurring work that must be budgeted explicitly.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: When planning Rust work, include the recurring quality follow-up if the current slice cannot cover docs, tests, benchmarks, or dependency review fully.
anti_pattern: // One-time cleanup complete; no follow-up needed.
good_pattern: // Follow-up: add proptest fixtures for parser edge cases after the first deterministic tests land.

source: Public Rust quality talk: Towards Impeccable Rust, maintenance section.
rule_summary: Make stagnation a deliberate choice, not the default. Automate dependency and toolchain update reminders, and document why an update is skipped.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: If you leave dependencies, toolchains, or lints stale, record the reason and ensure automation will remind maintainers again.
anti_pattern: // We have not updated dependencies in a year; nobody noticed.
good_pattern: // Dependabot tracks routine bumps. Holding foo 1.2 because 1.3 breaks MSRV; revisit after MSRV moves.

source: Public Rust quality talk: Towards Impeccable Rust, API design section.
rule_summary: `You are holding it wrong` is not an acceptable API answer. Make the wrong use hard or impossible and the right use obvious.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: If a caller can easily misuse your API, redesign the API before blaming caller discipline.
anti_pattern: fn commit(tx: Transaction, already_validated: bool) -> Result<()> { /* caller must know */ }

source: Public Rust quality talk: Towards Impeccable Rust, decision documentation and ADR guidance.
rule_summary: Document decisions while context is fresh, especially rejected alternatives and accepted downsides.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: For non-obvious Rust design choices, write down what alternatives were rejected and why. Future maintainers need the discarded paths more than the final code shape.
anti_pattern:
// Uses BTreeMap.
let entries = BTreeMap::new();
good_pattern:
// Decision: BTreeMap over HashMap because output order is part of snapshot stability.
// Rejected HashMap + sort because every query would pay the sort cost.

source: Public Rust quality talk: Towards Impeccable Rust, `Document what's not there` section.
rule_summary: Document what is not handled: shortcuts, missing corner cases, missing trait impls, and future optimization opportunities.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: Do not leave partial behavior invisible. Mark unhandled cases explicitly with documentation, TODOs, or `todo!()`/`unimplemented!()` where appropriate.

source: Public Rust quality talk: Towards Impeccable Rust; rust-imap `Seq` and `Uid` pattern.
rule_summary: Use newtypes, not type aliases, when the unit or meaning matters. Type aliases do not prevent accidental mixing.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: If two values share a primitive representation but differ semantically, make them distinct newtypes.

source: Public Rust quality talk: Towards Impeccable Rust; rust-imap `Client<T>` to `Session<T>` pattern.
rule_summary: Encode lifecycle phases as different types. Legal methods live only on the phase where they can be called, and fallible transitions should return the original value on failure when retry is possible.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: Do not represent lifecycle validity with booleans when distinct Rust types can make invalid calls fail to compile.
anti_pattern:
struct Rocket { in_air: bool }
impl Rocket { fn land(&mut self) { assert!(self.in_air); } }

source: Public Rust quality talk: Towards Impeccable Rust, two-phase structs example.
rule_summary: Use separate types for raw user configuration and resolved, defaulted, validated configuration.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: Do not let unvalidated configuration masquerade as validated configuration. Cross the boundary through a fallible conversion.
anti_pattern:
struct Config { path: String, timeout_ms: Option<u64> }
fn run(config: Config) -> Result<()> { /* re-checks everywhere */ }

source: Public Rust quality talk: Towards Impeccable Rust, enums-over-booleans section.
rule_summary: Prefer named enum variants over boolean parameters so call sites are readable and exhaustive.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: Replace boolean parameters with small enums whenever the call-site meaning is not obvious.
anti_pattern: open(path, true, false, true)?;
good_pattern: open(path, Create::Yes, Truncate::No, SyncWrites::Yes)?;

source: Public Rust quality talk: Towards Impeccable Rust, linked-argument enums section.
rule_summary: When arguments must vary together, encode the legal combinations as one enum instead of independent parameters.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: If only some combinations of parameters are valid, collapse them into a type that represents only those combinations.
anti_pattern: fn cache(enabled: bool, ttl: Option<Duration>) {} // false + Some is illegal
good_pattern:
enum CacheMode { Disabled, Enabled { ttl: Duration } }
fn cache(mode: CacheMode) {}

source: Helsing `sguaba` crate docs and frame-of-reference safety explanation.
rule_summary: In rare high-stakes domains, `unsafe` can signpost semantic obligations such as coordinate frames or units, concentrating auditable promises even when memory safety is not at risk.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: Only use semantic `unsafe` for catastrophic non-memory invariants, and document the exact semantic promise the caller upholds.
anti_pattern: fn rigid_body_transform(frame: Frame, data: Matrix) -> Transform { Transform { frame, data } }

source: `left-right` source; unsafe blocks carry invariant comments.
rule_summary: Every unsafe block needs a nearby `// SAFETY:` comment explaining why the invariants are upheld, not merely why unsafe is needed.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: An unsafe block without a reviewable `// SAFETY:` invariant explanation is not acceptable.
anti_pattern:
// Need unsafe for raw pointer.
let value = unsafe { *ptr };
good_pattern:
// SAFETY: `ptr` points into `storage`; readers cannot advance the epoch until this guard drops.
let value = unsafe { *ptr };

source: Public Rust quality talk: Towards Impeccable Rust, Clippy and Rust API Guidelines section.
rule_summary: Treat Clippy and the Rust API Guidelines as design feedback. Rust that smells like OOP, Python, Java, or C often fights the language.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: Do not dismiss lints as cosmetic until you can explain why the lint's design concern does not apply.
anti_pattern:
#[allow(clippy::all)]
mod new_feature;
good_pattern:
#[expect(clippy::too_many_lines, reason = "CLI dispatch is clearer as one match")]
fn dispatch() { /* ... */ }

source: Public Rust quality talk: Towards Impeccable Rust, stable-core/public-surface section.
rule_summary: Minimize public API. Prefer hiding implementation details with private fields, non-public helpers, and `impl Trait` returns where the concrete type is not part of the contract.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: Every `pub` item is a commitment. Before adding it, prove external users need it and that its exposed dependencies are stable enough.
anti_pattern:
pub struct Server { pub db: rusqlite::Connection }
impl From<External> for Server { /* public forever */ }
good_pattern:
pub struct Server { db: Database }
impl Server { pub fn stats(&self) -> Stats { self.db.stats() } }

source: Public Rust quality talk: Towards Impeccable Rust, dependencies section.
rule_summary: Any dependency type exposed in your public API becomes part of your semver surface. Wrap unstable or fast-moving dependency types before exposing them.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: Do not expose third-party types in public signatures casually; doing so couples your crate's versioning to theirs.
anti_pattern: pub fn connection(&self) -> &rusqlite::Connection { &self.conn }
good_pattern:
pub struct DbConnection { inner: rusqlite::Connection }
pub fn connection(&self) -> &DbConnection { &self.conn }

source: Public Rust quality talk: Towards Impeccable Rust, cargo-semver-checks and cargo-public-api guidance.
rule_summary: Use automation such as `cargo-semver-checks` and `cargo-public-api` to catch public API breakage humans miss.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: For public API changes, add or run compatibility tooling instead of relying on reviewer memory.
anti_pattern: // Public API changed; reviewer will notice if it is breaking.
good_pattern:
cargo semver-checks check-release
cargo public-api diff

source: Public Rust library style observed in rust-imap public API.
rule_summary: Use `#[non_exhaustive]` on public enums and structs unless you are certain the shape is final.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: New public data types need either `#[non_exhaustive]` or an explicit frozen-API justification.
anti_pattern: pub enum Status { Ready, Busy }
good_pattern:
#[non_exhaustive]
pub enum Status { Ready, Busy }

source: Observed public Rust repositories: rust-imap, inferno, fantoccini, and left-right.
rule_summary: Library crates should deny missing docs so every public item has a documented contract.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: Do not add public API without documentation, and prefer making missing docs a hard failure in library crates.
anti_pattern: pub fn parse(input: &str) -> Result<Value> { /* undocumented */ }

source: Direct inspection of Observed public Rust repositories: inferno, fantoccini, rust-imap, left-right.
rule_summary: Adopt strict crate-level lints: missing docs, Rust idioms, missing Debug, unreachable public items, and broken intra-doc links.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: New library crates should start with strict documentation and API-shape lints rather than adding them after the API drifts.
anti_pattern: #![allow(warnings)]
good_pattern:
#![deny(missing_docs)]
#![warn(rust_2018_idioms, missing_debug_implementations, unreachable_pub, broken_intra_doc_links)]

source: Public Rust crate metadata convention across public crates.
rule_summary: Use the Rust ecosystem standard `MIT OR Apache-2.0` dual license unless there is an intentional reason not to.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: Treat crate licensing as a deliberate compatibility decision, not an afterthought.
anti_pattern: license = "MIT" # chosen accidentally
good_pattern: license = "MIT OR Apache-2.0"

source: Public Rust quality talk: Towards Impeccable Rust, testing section.
rule_summary: Assert everything that must be true. In tests and invariant-heavy code, assertions turn silent drift into local failures.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: If correctness depends on an invariant, assert it where it is established or consumed.
anti_pattern: self.len -= 1; // assumes len was non-zero
good_pattern:
debug_assert!(self.len > 0, "pop called only after non-empty check");
self.len -= 1;

source: Public Rust quality talk: Towards Impeccable Rust; left-right safety workflow.
rule_summary: Run tests under Miri for unsafe or subtle code so undefined behavior surfaces even when normal tests pass.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: For unsafe code, pointer-heavy code, or complex aliasing, `cargo test` is not enough. Add or document a `cargo miri test` path.
anti_pattern: // Unsafe pointer code is covered by regular unit tests only.
good_pattern: cargo +nightly miri test

source: left-right `.github/workflows/safety.yml` sanitizer workflow.
rule_summary: Run sanitizer-backed tests, especially ASan and LSan, when memory behavior or unsafe code matters.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: For unsafe or systems-adjacent code, consider sanitizer CI alongside normal tests and Miri.
anti_pattern: // Memory safety checked only by review.
good_pattern: RUSTFLAGS="-Z sanitizer=address" cargo +nightly test -Z build-std

source: Public Rust quality talk: Towards Impeccable Rust, error-case testing aside.
rule_summary: Test error paths, not only happy paths. Rarely exercised failure branches are where production bugs hide.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: Any new fallible behavior needs tests for at least one success path and the important failure paths.
anti_pattern:
#[test]
fn parses_valid_config() { assert!(parse("ok").is_ok()); }
good_pattern:
#[test]
fn rejects_missing_required_field() { assert!(matches!(parse("{}"), Err(Error::MissingField(_)))); }

source: Public Rust quality talk and public Compose podcast discussion.
rule_summary: Use the right chaos tool for the risk: `turmoil` or `shuttle` for scheduling, `proptest` or `quickcheck` for input spaces, and `cargo-mutants` to test the tests.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: For high-stakes logic, name which chaos or mutation technique would expose bugs that example tests cannot.
anti_pattern: // One hand-written concurrent test is enough.
good_pattern: // Add shuttle model test for task interleavings and proptest for generated request sequences.

source: Public Rust quality talk: Towards Impeccable Rust, Loom and Kani recommendations.
rule_summary: Where exhaustive exploration is affordable, use it. Loom explores thread interleavings; Kani can prove input-space properties.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: For concurrent or unsafe code, ask whether Loom or Kani can give a stronger guarantee than sampled tests.
anti_pattern: // Threading code tested once with two spawned threads.

source: Public Compose podcast discussion; repeated proptest/quickcheck recommendations.
rule_summary: Use property tests to assert relationships across large input spaces, especially against a simpler reference implementation when available.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: If the behavior has algebraic, round-trip, ordering, or equivalence properties, add property tests rather than only examples.
anti_pattern:
#[test]
fn one_case() { assert_eq!(fast(vec![1, 2]), slow(vec![1, 2])); }

source: Public Compose podcast discussion, concolic execution discussion.
rule_summary: For high-stakes input spaces, consider concolic or symbolic execution to find adversarial cases random generation may never hit.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: When random property testing is too weak for the risk, consider symbolic or concolic exploration and document why it is or is not practical.
anti_pattern: // Random tests did not find anything, therefore all inputs are safe.
good_pattern: // Kani proof covers all u16 state transitions; proptest remains as a regression harness.

source: Public Compose podcast discussion, chaos testing discussion.
rule_summary: Kill things on purpose and verify recovery. For libraries, inject I/O and dependency failures at arbitrary points.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: If code handles external systems, add tests where those systems fail, stall, or return malformed data.
anti_pattern: // File writes are assumed to succeed in tests.
good_pattern:
let fs = FailingFs::fail_after(2);
assert!(matches!(save_with_fs(&fs, data), Err(Error::Write(_))));

source: Public Rust quality talk: Towards Impeccable Rust, benchmarks section.
rule_summary: Benchmarks should cover the whole performance shape: pathological inputs, micro and macro paths, under/at/over capacity, and relevant target platforms.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: Do not benchmark only the easy common case when claiming performance. Cover the shape of the workload.
anti_pattern: // Benchmarked one small happy-path input, so the parser is faster.
good_pattern: // Benchmarks include tiny, median fixture, huge file, deeply nested syntax, and malformed recovery cases.

source: Public Rust quality talk: Towards Impeccable Rust, trustworthy measurements section.
rule_summary: Trustworthy measurements matter more than precise-looking noisy numbers. Prefer instruction counts or interleaved comparisons, and reduce ambient noise.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: Do not base performance claims on one wall-clock loop. Use a benchmark method that controls noise and compares fairly.
good_pattern: // iai-callgrind tracks instruction counts; tango interleaves old/new runs for fair comparison.

source: Public Rust quality talk: Towards Impeccable Rust, benchmark regression CI section.
rule_summary: Benchmarks must fail CI on meaningful regressions, or they become decorative and easy to ignore.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: If performance matters enough to benchmark, wire regression detection into review or CI.
anti_pattern: // Benchmark output is posted somewhere but nobody checks it.
good_pattern: // CI rejects >5% instruction-count regression on the indexed hot path.

source: Public Rust quality talk: Towards Impeccable Rust, `Speed isn't all` section.
rule_summary: Measure what matters, not only speed: throughput, goodput, memory, tail latency, and real-world correctness outcomes.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: Performance evidence must name the metric that matters to users, not only a faster mean runtime.
anti_pattern: // Mean runtime improved, so the change is good.
good_pattern: // p99 latency dropped from 180ms to 70ms; memory max stayed under 256MiB; output accuracy unchanged.

source: Public Rust quality talk: Towards Impeccable Rust, benchmark workload-shape warning.
rule_summary: Simple loop benchmarks lie. Workload model, representativeness, recording method, and statistical comparison determine whether the result means anything.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: Do not accept `new > old` as a benchmark without workload shape and statistical rigor.
anti_pattern: // Ran both versions once in a loop; the new number is bigger.
good_pattern: // Open-loop load at 50/100/200 rps; CDF reports p50/p95/p99; comparison uses confidence intervals.

source: Public Rust quality talk: Towards Impeccable Rust, dependencies section.
rule_summary: Know every dependency you ship, including transitive versions, and keep the inventory machine-readable.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: Treat the dependency closure as part of the product. You should be able to answer which deployed versions include a vulnerable crate.
anti_pattern: // Cargo.lock exists somewhere; inventory is manual.
good_pattern:
cargo tree --locked
cargo auditable build

source: Public Rust quality talk: Towards Impeccable Rust, RUSTSEC/cargo-audit recommendation.
rule_summary: Connect dependencies to known issues automatically with RUSTSEC or `cargo-audit` in CI.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: Known dependency vulnerabilities should surface in CI, not only when someone remembers to audit manually.
anti_pattern: // Security review happens before releases if we remember.
good_pattern: cargo audit --deny warnings

source: Public Rust quality talk: Towards Impeccable Rust, cargo-vet recommendation.
rule_summary: Use `cargo-vet` to accumulate review evidence for crates and versions, covering unknown issues beyond known CVEs.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: For serious software, record dependency review decisions so trust accumulates instead of evaporating between releases.
anti_pattern: // Alice looked at that crate once, probably.
good_pattern: cargo vet certify important-crate 1.2.3 --criteria safe-to-deploy

source: Public Rust quality talk: Towards Impeccable Rust, upstream changes/no forks guidance.
rule_summary: Wrap unstable dependencies behind a thin boundary instead of forking or leaking their churn through your core API.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: If a dependency is unstable or fast-moving, isolate it behind your own module or crate before it infects the public API.
anti_pattern: pub use unstable_crate::Client;
good_pattern:
pub struct Client { inner: unstable_crate::Client }
impl Client { pub fn request(&self, request: Request) -> Result<Response> { /* ... */ } }

source: thesquareplanet.com Rust Tips and Tricks.
rule_summary: Omit `return` for final expressions in functions, blocks, match arms, and closures.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: Use Rust's expression-oriented style for final values unless early return is intended.

source: thesquareplanet.com Rust Tips and Tricks.
rule_summary: Pass methods or functions directly to iterator adapters when that reads clearer than a closure.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: Prefer direct function items over closures that only forward the call.
anti_pattern: let lengths: Vec<_> = words.iter().map(|word| word.len()).collect();
good_pattern: let lengths: Vec<_> = words.iter().map(String::len).collect();

source: thesquareplanet.com Rust Tips and Tricks.
rule_summary: Use `todo!()` or `unimplemented!()` for not-yet-written code and `unreachable!()` for impossible paths; avoid `panic!("TODO")`.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: Use the specific standard macro that communicates the intent of a hole or impossible branch.
anti_pattern: panic!("TODO");
good_pattern: todo!("support sparse workspace manifests")

source: thesquareplanet.com Rust Tips and Tricks.
rule_summary: Use `Option::as_ref()` when you have `&Option<T>` and need `Option<&T>` for borrowing-oriented combinators.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: Avoid extra pattern-matching indentation when `as_ref`, `map`, and `and_then` express the optional borrow directly.
good_pattern: self.value.as_ref().map(Value::render).unwrap_or_else(default)

source: thesquareplanet.com Rust Tips and Tricks.
rule_summary: Use the `Entry` API for check-then-insert or insert-or-update map logic.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: Replace `contains_key`/`get_mut`/`insert` dances with `entry` when updating maps.
anti_pattern:
if !map.contains_key(&key) { map.insert(key.clone(), Vec::new()); }
map.get_mut(&key).unwrap().push(value);
good_pattern: map.entry(key).or_insert_with(Vec::new).push(value);

source: thesquareplanet.com Rust Tips and Tricks.
rule_summary: Prefer `let x: Vec<_> = iter.collect();` over turbofish when the binding type communicates the container shape more clearly.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: Let the binding carry the collection type when that makes `collect` easier to read.
anti_pattern: let numbers = (0..10).collect::<Vec<_>>();
good_pattern: let numbers: Vec<_> = (0..10).collect();

source: thesquareplanet.com Rust Tips and Tricks.
rule_summary: Use `.enumerate()` when you need both an index and an element. Avoid manual counters and index loops over collections.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: Do not write manual counters or `0..len` loops when iteration over elements is what you mean.

source: thesquareplanet.com Rust Tips and Tricks.
rule_summary: Use `.cloned()` when cloning every element of an iterator over references.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: Prefer the adapter that names the intent over a closure that only calls `clone`.
anti_pattern: let owned: Vec<_> = items.iter().map(|item| item.clone()).collect();
good_pattern: let owned: Vec<_> = items.iter().cloned().collect();

source: thesquareplanet.com Rust Tips and Tricks.
rule_summary: Use `_` for intentionally unused arguments and `_name` when the unused value still deserves a name. Avoid broad unused-variable allows.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: Mark unused parameters locally and intentionally instead of hiding unused-variable warnings for whole functions or modules.
anti_pattern:
#[allow(unused_variables)]
fn handle(request: Request, debug: bool) { /* ... */ }
good_pattern: fn handle(request: Request, _debug: bool) { /* debug intentionally unused in release path */ }

source: Public Rust trivia thread, mutually exclusive reference framing.
rule_summary: Think of `&mut T` as a mutually exclusive reference, not merely a mutable one. The key property is uniqueness of access.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: When reasoning about borrow errors, ask what other access overlaps the exclusive reference, even if the code only reads.

source: Public Rust trivia thread collected by rusty-ferris-club.
rule_summary: Know small standard-library tools that prevent bugs: `Option` as an iterator, formatter debug builders, `TryFrom` over narrowing `as`, `TcpStream::set_nodelay`, and appropriate atomics.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: Before hand-rolling common patterns, verify whether the standard library already has the safer idiom.
anti_pattern: let small = big as u8; // may truncate silently
good_pattern: let small = u8::try_from(big).expect("value was range-checked before conversion");

source: left-right crate-level docs trade-offs section.
rule_summary: Document design tradeoffs and drawbacks in crate-level docs so users and contributors understand what is intentionally non-negotiable.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: If the design sacrifices memory, determinism, write speed, ergonomics, or scope, say so explicitly where users will see it.
anti_pattern:
//! Fast cache library.
//! No mention that writes are single-threaded and memory is doubled.

source: left-right and sguaba documentation patterns.
rule_summary: Link to deeper explanations instead of overloading inline docs. API docs should be sufficient to use the crate; background can live elsewhere.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: When a concept is too deep for inline docs, link authoritative background rather than burying a tutorial in a code comment.
anti_pattern: /// 200 lines explaining the entire algorithm before a small public type.
good_pattern:
/// Implements the two-handle read path described in the crate-level `Trade-offs` section.
/// See the linked design note for the full algorithm.

source: left-right and rust-imap crate-level examples.
rule_summary: Examples should show the full shape of real API use, not only a tiny hello-world path that hides setup and teardown.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: Public examples should make the real cost and shape of using the API visible.

source: Public JetBrains interview and Rust forum follow-ups on LLMs.
rule_summary: LLMs do best on pattern-matched work with references, tests, or feedback loops; they are risky for genuinely novel algorithms or unverifiable claims.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: Before using an LLM-generated design, identify the reference implementation, tests, docs, or verifier that can catch plausible nonsense.
anti_pattern: // Agent invented a new algorithm and the output looks plausible.
good_pattern: // Agent patch is checked against existing tests, a reference implementation, and a property-test comparison.

source: Public late-2025 stream and `trough` PR audit workflow.
rule_summary: Use LLMs as review, audit, and polish passes when possible; keep design authorship and acceptance decisions with the human.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: For important Rust design, do not let the model be the only designer. Use it to challenge, audit, and refine evidence-backed work.
anti_pattern: // Model wrote the design; merged because it compiled.
good_pattern: // Human-authored design; model audit produced findings, each accepted only after local verification.

source: Public Rust forum replies and Helsing Sguaba blog post.
rule_summary: Rust can prevent business-logic bugs only when you choose APIs and libraries that encode domain invariants in types.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: Do not assume Rust automatically prevents domain bugs. Architect with typed invariants, or the bug remains a runtime concern.
anti_pattern: fn transform(point: [f64; 3], from_frame: &str, to_frame: &str) -> [f64; 3] { /* ... */ }
good_pattern:
struct Point<Frame> { coords: [f64; 3], frame: PhantomData<Frame> }
fn transform<A, B>(point: Point<A>, transform: Transform<A, B>) -> Point<B> { /* ... */ }

source: Rust for Rustaceans, ch. 4 §Error Source
rule_summary: Library error variants that wrap an upstream error must implement `Error::source()` so callers can walk the cause chain. Do not flatten the underlying cause into a `Display` string.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: When an error variant wraps another error, return the wrapped error from `source()`. Never collapse the cause into a formatted string in `Display`; the message describes this layer's failure, the chain carries the rest.

source: Rust for Rustaceans, ch. 4 §Error Conversion
rule_summary: Use `?` (with `From`) to propagate errors through layers that share a domain. Use `map_err` only at module or domain boundaries where the calling layer needs different context. Do not apply both at the same call site without justification.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: `?` and `map_err` answer different questions. Use `?` when the caller's domain matches yours. Use `map_err` only when crossing into a layer that owns a different error type. If you find yourself adding context strings inside one domain, extend the underlying error variant instead.

source: Rust for Rustaceans, ch. 4 §Error Display; Rust API Guidelines C-WORD-ORDER.
rule_summary: Error variant names are nouns naming the failure (`InvalidUtf8`, `MissingField`), not verb phrases (`FailedToParseUtf8`). `Display` produces one short clause without trailing punctuation and without leaking internal state callers cannot use.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: Name error variants for the failure condition, not the operation that failed. Format `Display` output as a noun-phrase or short clause: lowercase, no trailing period, no struct dumps. Internal detail belongs in `Debug` or in `source()` chains.

source: Rust for Rustaceans, ch. 4 §Error Hierarchies
rule_summary: Distinguish failure categories — I/O, parse, domain, and invariant violations — as distinct variants or sub-enums. Never collapse them into a single `Other(String)` or `Internal(String)` catch-all.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: Each error variant should let a caller decide whether to retry, surface, or abort. Variants that erase that distinction (`Other(String)`, `Internal(String)`) push the work back to the caller through string parsing. Add a real variant or sub-enum instead.

source: Rust for Rustaceans, ch. 4 §Backtraces
rule_summary: Capture `std::backtrace::Backtrace` only at the boundary where stack context would otherwise be lost — typically the lowest-level error variant or the first conversion away from the originating frame. Avoid capturing on every wrap.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: Backtraces are expensive (a stack walk plus optional symbolication). Capture them once, at the deepest layer that owns the original failure, not at every wrapping site. Application code is more permissive than library code; libraries should let the caller opt in.

source: Rust for Rustaceans, ch. 4 §Error Stability and ch. 8 §Versioning Surface
rule_summary: `#[non_exhaustive]` lets you add variants without a major bump, but does not document which existing variants callers may match on as a stable contract. Document each variant's stability explicitly.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: Treat each public error variant as either a stable contract (callers may match on it) or an internal-detail variant (rendered through `Display` only, may be split or removed). Document the distinction in rustdoc; do not let it default to ambiguity.

source: Rust for Rustaceans, ch. 4 §Carrying Context
rule_summary: Carry error context as typed fields on the variant, not as `format!`-built strings. Reserve `eyre`/`anyhow` chained-string context for application code where the caller never needs to inspect the parts.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: If a caller might want to programmatically inspect what failed (which path, which line, which key), put that information in fields on the error variant. `format!`-built context strings discard the data and force string parsing later.

source: Rust for Rustaceans, ch. 4 §Library Error Types
rule_summary: Library code should not use `Box<dyn Error>` even in private helpers: it leaks anyhow-style erasure into the call graph and prevents callers (including your own code) from reasoning about variants. Build the structured type once and use it everywhere.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: If you have a structured `Error` enum for the public API, use it in private helpers too. `Box<dyn Error>` in a private signature is a tell that you have not finished designing the variant; finish the design.

source: Rust for Rustaceans, ch. 4 §From Conversions and ch. 8 §Public Surface
rule_summary: Every `From<E>` for your error type widens the conversion graph and becomes part of the public API: callers' types now flow through `?` into yours. Review each one as a deliberate API decision, not as a convenience for `?`.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: Adding `impl From<UpstreamError> for MyError` is a public API change even when the wrapping variant is internal-detail. Decide whether the upstream error genuinely belongs in your domain before adding the conversion.

source: Rust for Rustaceans, ch. 6 §Testing Errors
rule_summary: Tests of failure paths must assert on the specific error variant, not just `is_err()`. A test that passes for any failure cannot tell when a refactor silently flips one failure cause into another.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: For every fallible behavior under test, assert `matches!(result, Err(Error::ExpectedVariant(..)))` or destructure to inspect fields. `assert!(result.is_err())` lets two unrelated bugs satisfy the same test.

source: Rust for Rustaceans, ch. 1 §Lifetimes; Crust of Rust: Lifetime Annotations
rule_summary: A borrow ends at its last use, not at the end of the curly-brace scope. When the borrow checker rejects code, restructure (move the conflicting use earlier or use the value sooner) before reaching for `.clone()` or owned types.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: When the borrow checker rejects code that conflicts with a live borrow, first ask whether the borrow's last use can move earlier. NLL means a borrow's effective scope is its live range, not its lexical block. `.clone()` is the last resort, not the first.

source: Rust for Rustaceans, ch. 1 §Borrowing; Crust of Rust: Lifetime Annotations
rule_summary: Passing a `&mut T` to a function does not move the reference; the compiler reborrows it as `&mut *m` for the call's duration, after which the original is usable again. Lean on this rather than working around perceived double-borrows.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: When chaining calls on a `&mut T`, trust the compiler's reborrowing. `helper(buf); buf.push(x);` works because `helper` got a reborrow `&mut *buf`, not the original. Manual `std::mem::take` / `std::mem::replace` gymnastics are a code smell when reborrowing would suffice.

source: Rust for Rustaceans, ch. 1 §Ownership and Borrowing
rule_summary: `&mut T` is a unique borrow, not ownership. You cannot move out of the borrowed value (consume it) without leaving valid data behind; you can mutate in place, and you can replace via `*ref = new` or `mem::replace`. APIs that need to consume the value entirely should take `T` (owned); APIs that mutate-and-return-control should take `&mut T`.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: Choose between `&mut T` and `T` by what the API does to the value: mutate-in-place takes `&mut T`; consume / replace / extract takes `T`. `drop(buf)` on a `buf: &mut T` only drops the reference, not the value — usually a sign the wrong signature was chosen.

source: Rust for Rustaceans, ch. 1 §Variance and Subtyping; Rustonomicon §Variance; Crust of Rust: Subtyping and Variance
rule_summary: Pick the `PhantomData<...>` marker that captures what your type semantically holds, because variance determines soundness. Per the Rustonomicon variance table: `&'a T` is covariant in `'a` and `T`; `&'a mut T` is covariant in `'a` and invariant in `T`; `fn(T)` is contravariant in `T`; `fn() -> T` is covariant in `T`; `*const T` is covariant in `T`; `*mut T` is invariant in `T`. `PhantomData<X>` mirrors `X`.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: When defining a struct that conceptually holds a borrow, raw pointer, or function involving a generic parameter it does not store as a field, choose the `PhantomData<...>` marker by variance, not by convenience. The marker dictates whether `'short` lifetimes can subtype `'long` and whether `T` may be substituted for a subtype, which determines whether your unsafe abstraction is sound.

source: Rust for Rustaceans, ch. 1 §Drop Check; Rust Reference §Destructors
rule_summary: Struct fields are dropped in declaration order (top to bottom). Design the field order so that fields holding borrows or callbacks into other fields are declared *before* the fields they depend on — dependents drop first, dependencies drop last.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: Order struct fields by drop dependency: a field that uses another field in its `Drop` impl must be declared first. If `logger.flush()` writes to `writer`, declare `logger` before `writer` so logger's destructor runs while writer is still alive.

source: Rust for Rustaceans, ch. 1 §Self-Referential Types; Crust of Rust: Pin
rule_summary: Naive self-referential structs (`struct { data: String, view: &str_into_data }`) do not compile, and faking `'static` is unsound the moment `data` moves. Use indices into the owning data, an arena (`bumpalo`, `typed-arena`), or a Pin-based crate (`ouroboros`, `self_cell`) instead.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: If your struct conceptually holds a reference into a field of itself, the design is wrong before any code compiles. Replace the inner reference with an index, restructure ownership through an arena, or pull in `ouroboros`/`self_cell`. Never `'static`-cast the inner reference; moving the outer struct will dangle it.

source: Rust for Rustaceans, ch. 1 §Interior Mutability; Crust of Rust: Smart Pointers
rule_summary: Pick the lightest interior-mutability primitive that satisfies the sharing need: `Cell<T>` (single-threaded; `Copy` required for `get`; `set`/`replace`/`swap`/`take` available for non-`Copy` types; no runtime borrow check), `RefCell<T>` (single-threaded, compound types, runtime borrow check), atomics (`AtomicU64` etc., thread-shared primitives, lock-free), `Mutex<T>` / `RwLock<T>` (thread-shared compound types, blocking).
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: Do not reach for `Mutex<T>` reflexively when the sharing pattern is single-threaded or primitive. `Cell<T>` and `RefCell<T>` are single-thread and free of locking; atomics are thread-shared and lock-free; `Mutex`/`RwLock` are thread-shared and lock-bearing. Picking a heavier primitive than required is a wasted-cost smell.

source: Rust for Rustaceans, ch. 5 §Async; Tokio docs §Shared State; Crust of Rust: Channels
rule_summary: A `std::sync::MutexGuard` held across an `.await` point makes the surrounding future `!Send` (the guard is unconditionally `!Send` due to OS thread-affinity for unlock). On `Send`-bound executors (e.g. `tokio::spawn`), this is a compile error. On `!Send` executors, holding a sync lock across await wastes the executor thread and risks deadlock when another task on the same executor needs the lock. Drop the guard before awaiting, or use `tokio::sync::Mutex` (whose guard is `Send` and integrates with the runtime).
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: Never hold a `std::sync::Mutex` (or `RwLock`) guard across an `.await`. Either narrow the locked region so the guard drops before the await, or replace the lock with `tokio::sync::Mutex` for genuine async-aware locking. The compiler will not always catch this; write the code so the guard's scope is local.

source: Rust for Rustaceans, ch. 1 §Pinning and ch. 5 §Async; Crust of Rust: Pin
rule_summary: Most types are `Unpin` (the auto trait); you only need to think about `Pin` when designing a type that is self-referential or implementing `Future::poll` directly. `Pin<&mut Self>` in `poll` means the future's memory address is stable so any internal self-references stay valid; respect that contract or use `pin_project`.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: When implementing `Future::poll` directly or designing a self-referential type, take the `Pin<&mut Self>` contract seriously: do not move out of `self`, do not return a `&mut` to a self-referential field without `pin_project`, and do not assume the type is `Unpin` unless you have an explicit `impl Unpin for ...` with documented soundness. For everything else, normal `&mut self` methods are fine because most types are auto-`Unpin`.

source: Rust for Rustaceans, ch. 2 §Coherence; rust-imap pattern; Rust API Guidelines C-SEALED
rule_summary: Use a private super trait to seal a public trait: external crates can call methods on it but cannot implement it. Pattern: a private module containing a `Sealed` trait, then `pub trait Foo: private::Sealed {}` plus `impl private::Sealed for ...` only for the types you control.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: When you publish a trait whose set of implementors must remain bounded (closed-set extensions over std types, library-controlled implementations only), seal it with a private super trait. Without sealing, every minor-version method addition risks breaking external implementors.

source: Rust Reference §Object Safety; Crust of Rust: Trait Objects
rule_summary:
A trait is object-safe (dyn-compatible) only if every method either has a dispatchable receiver and no generic type parameters, or is excluded from the vtable via `where Self: Sized`. Dispatchable receivers: `&self`, `&mut self`, `Box`, `Rc`, `Arc`, `Pin

`. A consuming `self` receiver does not by itself make the trait non-dyn-compatible — the trait still satisfies dyn-compatibility, but the consuming method is not callable through `&dyn` (only through `Box` or via `where Self: Sized` gating).
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: If callers may use your trait as `dyn Trait`, design every method to be dispatchable (no generic params, dispatchable receiver, no Self by value) or gate it behind `where Self: Sized` so it is excluded from the vtable. Adding a generic method later silently breaks `dyn Trait` usage at every call site.

source: Rust for Rustaceans, ch. 2 §Trait Objects and Object Safety
rule_summary: Design traits to be object-safe by default; give it up only when the trait is genuinely a static-only abstraction. For traits exposed to user-defined plugins or heterogeneous collections, object safety is required. Provide generic conveniences via a separate extension trait with a blanket impl, so the core stays dyn-compatible.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: When designing a public trait, decide upfront whether `dyn Trait` is in scope. If yes, keep methods object-safe and put generic helpers on a separate `*Ext` extension trait with `impl<T: BaseTrait + ?Sized> Ext for T {}`. If no, document the decision in rustdoc — callers cannot store `Box<dyn YourTrait>`.

source: Rust for Rustaceans, ch. 2 §Generics vs Trait Objects; Rust Book ch. 17
rule_summary: Pick generics (`fn foo<T: Trait>(x: T)`) for hot paths and homogeneous types: monomorphization, inlining, no indirect call. Pick trait objects (`fn foo(x: &dyn Trait)`) for heterogeneous collections, plugin boundaries, and binary-size budgets: single code copy, vtable indirection. Mixing both in one API is rarely the right answer.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: Default to generics for performance-sensitive code that handles a single type per call site. Use `dyn Trait` only when you need heterogeneous collections, plugin boundaries that decouple consumers from concrete types, or to keep binary size bounded. Document the choice when it is non-obvious.

source: Rust for Rustaceans, ch. 2 §Associated Types vs Generics; Rust Book ch. 19
rule_summary: Use an associated type when the trait has one logical impl per type (`Iterator::Item` is determined by the iterator). Use a generic parameter when the caller picks (`Add<RHS>` lets one type implement addition with multiple right-hand-side types). Don't mix both unless the relation requires it.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: Decide for each trait whether the related type is determined by the implementor (associated type) or chosen by the caller (generic parameter). If implementors have exactly one natural choice, an associated type spares callers from spelling it out. If multiple choices coexist on one type, a generic parameter is necessary.

source: Rust for Rustaceans, ch. 2 §HRTBs; Rust Reference §Higher-Rank Trait Bounds; Crust of Rust: Lifetime Annotations
rule_summary: `for<'a> Fn(&'a T) -> R` (and the related `for<'a> Fn(&'a T) -> &'a U` form when the return borrows from the input) declares the callback works for any caller-chosen lifetime, not a single named lifetime fixed at the bound site. Use HRTBs when a function passes borrowed data into a callback whose lifetime cannot be tied to the function's own type parameters.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: When a callback bound describes a closure or function pointer that the implementation will invoke with locally-borrowed data, write the bound as `for<'a> Fn(&'a T) -> ...` (HRTB). A single named lifetime tied to the outer function's type parameters constrains the callback to one specific lifetime, which usually fails when the implementation has only a shorter borrow to offer.

source: Rust for Rustaceans, ch. 2 §Sized and DSTs; Rustonomicon §Exotically Sized Types
rule_summary: Generic type parameters carry an implicit `Sized` bound. Relax it with `T: ?Sized` to accept dynamically-sized types (`str`, `[T]`, `dyn Trait`). The cost is that the parameter can only be used through references or owning pointers (`&T`, `&mut T`, `Box<T>`, `Arc<T>`, `Rc<T>`); never by value, since the size is unknown at compile time.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: For container or wrapper types that should accept DSTs, write the parameter as `T: ?Sized` and use it only through references or owning smart pointers. For parameters used by value (returned, moved, stored inline), keep the implicit `Sized` bound.

source: Rust for Rustaceans, ch. 2 §Zero-Sized Types; Rust Book ch. 5
rule_summary: A zero-sized type (`struct A;`, `()`, `PhantomData<T>`, structs with all-ZST fields) costs no memory at runtime but carries information at compile time. Use them for typestate phases, capability tokens, type-level booleans, and marker bounds. `Vec<()>` never allocates element storage — pushes only update the length counter.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: When you need a compile-time witness that an operation has happened or that a value belongs to a category, use a ZST. Unit structs, `PhantomData`, and ZST-only fields encode information in the type system at zero runtime cost.

source: Rust for Rustaceans, ch. 2 §Auto Traits and Markers; Rust Reference §Auto Traits
rule_summary: Auto traits (`Send`, `Sync`, `Unpin`, `UnwindSafe`, `RefUnwindSafe`) are inferred by the compiler from the fields of your type — `Sized` is implicit and special-cased by the compiler but is NOT an auto trait. Do not impl auto traits explicitly except `unsafe impl` for the rare type that wraps a raw pointer or other `!Send`/`!Sync` primitive whose external invariants you have audited. Non-auto coherence-bound traits require deliberate alignment when hand-implemented: `Hash` and `Eq` must agree (`a == b` implies `hash(a) == hash(b)`), and `Ord` and `PartialOrd` must agree (`cmp(a, b)` matches `partial_cmp(a, b).unwrap()`).
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: Do not explicitly impl auto traits for normal types — let the compiler propagate them from fields. Use `unsafe impl Send`/`Sync` only for types that wrap raw pointers or other `!Send`/`!Sync` primitives whose invariants you have audited. For non-auto coherence-bound traits (`Hash`/`Eq`, `Ord`/`PartialOrd`), keep manual impls in agreement so `a == b` implies `hash(a) == hash(b)` and `cmp(a, b)` implies `partial_cmp(a, b)`.

source: Rust for Rustaceans, ch. 9 §Allocation; Rust Performance Book §Heap Allocations
rule_summary: In hot paths, preallocate `Vec` and `String` with `with_capacity` for known sizes, avoid `format!`/`to_string` calls inside tight loops, and reuse buffers with `.clear()` rather than reallocating. Each `format!` allocates a fresh `String`; `write!` into a preallocated buffer with `std::fmt::Write` writes into the existing capacity (only reallocates if the buffer outgrows the initial estimate).
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: When a function runs in a hot path (per profile or by design), preallocate output buffers with `with_capacity`, route formatted output through `write!` against an existing buffer rather than `format!`, and reuse rather than recreate buffers across iterations. Each unjustified allocation in a hot loop is a measurable cost.

source: Rust for Rustaceans, ch. 1 §Cow; Rust Performance Book §Type Sizes; std docs §Box<str>
rule_summary: Pick the right string type by use case: `&'static str` for compile-time strings (no allocation), `&str` for borrowed slices, `Box<str>` for owned-but-not-growable (drops the capacity field — saves ~8 bytes per string vs `String`), `String` for owned+growable, `Cow<'a, str>` for sometimes-borrowed-sometimes-owned. Defaulting everything to `String` allocates where you don't need to.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: Choose the string type that matches the value's actual lifecycle. `String` is owned and growable — use it only when you need both. For struct fields known at compile time, use `&'static str`. For owned-but-frozen, use `Box<str>`. For sometimes-borrowed, use `Cow<'a, str>`. Excess `String` allocations compound across hot paths.

source: Rust Performance Book §Bounds Checks; Crust of Rust: Iterators
rule_summary: Use `iter()`/`iter_mut()` over indexed loops where possible. Iterators carry the structural guarantee that each element is touched exactly once, which lets the compiler reliably elide bounds checks, autovectorize, and inline aggressively. Indexed access (`arr[i]` in `for i in 0..arr.len()`) can also be optimized but the elision is conditional on the compiler proving `i < len`, which it doesn't always do for non-trivial loops.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: Default to `iter()`/`iter_mut()` for elementwise traversal, not indexed loops. The iterator form gives the compiler a structural proof of safety; the indexed form requires the compiler to derive that proof from the loop bounds. Reach for indexed access only when you genuinely need an index alongside the value (use `.enumerate()` instead in many cases).

source: Rust for Rustaceans, ch. 9 §Compile Times; Rust Performance Book §Type Sizes; cargo llvm-lines documentation
rule_summary: Each concrete instantiation of a generic function generates a separate copy in the binary. Hot path: generics win (inline, no indirection). Cold or rarely-used code with many instantiations: `dyn Trait` wins (single code copy, smaller binary, slightly slower call). Profile binary size with `cargo llvm-lines` or `cargo bloat` before introducing generics in cold code, and pair this rule with axiom 79 (when to switch to dyn).
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: Generics are not free — each new concrete `T` duplicates the function body. For cold or many-typed call sites, accept the indirect call cost of `dyn Trait` to keep binary size bounded. Profile, don't guess: `cargo llvm-lines` and `cargo bloat` show actual binary impact per generic.

source: Rust Reference §inline attribute; Rust Performance Book §Inlining
rule_summary: `#[inline]` *suggests* the compiler inline this function across crate boundaries (within a crate, the compiler decides regardless). `#[inline(always)]` is a *strong directive* — the compiler honors it in virtually all non-recursive cases, though it remains a hint and can be refused for recursive functions or some debug-build configurations. Rarely the right call: can hurt I-cache for large functions. `#[inline(never)]` disables inlining; useful for debugging codegen, profile attribution, or preserving symbols in stack traces.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: Use `#[inline]` on small, hot, cross-crate-callable functions. Reserve `#[inline(always)]` for functions you have measured to need it, not as a generic perf incantation. Use `#[inline(never)]` deliberately for debugging or to keep a function present in profiles and stack traces.

source: Rust for Rustaceans, ch. 2 §Trait Objects; enum_dispatch crate; Crust of Rust: Trait Objects
rule_summary: When the variant set is fixed at the library boundary (you control all impls), an enum dispatches faster than `Box<dyn Trait>`: no vtable, no indirect branch, the `match` compiles to direct branches or a jump table (depending on variant count) that the optimizer can specialize per arm. Use `dyn Trait` only when external code must add new variants. Caveat: when variants vary wildly in size, `Vec<MyEnum>` reserves space for the largest variant per element, so `Vec<Box<dyn Trait>>` (16-byte fat pointers) may use less memory — measure both for size-sensitive workloads. See also axiom 88 (monomorphization cost) for the open-set case where `dyn Trait` may still beat generics on binary size.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: Reach for `dyn Trait` only when external crates need to add their own implementors. For closed-set polymorphism (your library knows all variants), define an enum and dispatch via `match`. The enum form is faster (no indirect call), more inspectable (exhaustive matches surface missed variants at compile time), and often clearer to read.

source: Rust Reference §Type Layout; Rust Performance Book §Type Sizes
rule_summary: Rust's default `repr(Rust)` is allowed to reorder fields and does so in practice to minimize padding (the Reference makes few layout guarantees beyond size and alignment). `#[repr(C)]` (FFI, stable layout) freezes declaration order — manually order fields by *descending* alignment to avoid padding gaps. For hot-path structs accessed by separate cores, lay out with cache-line awareness (64-byte boundary on most x86-64; 128 on aarch64/Apple Silicon) to avoid false sharing.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: Trust `repr(Rust)` to minimize padding by default. When you must use `#[repr(C)]` (FFI, stable layout, hand-tuned layout), order fields by descending alignment so smaller fields fill the trailing space without inserting padding. For shared cache-line state, separate hot fields onto distinct lines; `crossbeam-utils::CachePadded<T>` is the standard helper.

source: Rust Reference §Atomics; Rust Performance Book §Atomics; Crust of Rust: Atomics
rule_summary: Pick the weakest ordering that satisfies your synchronization need. `Relaxed`: atomicity only, no ordering between threads (counters, stats). `Acquire` (loads) / `Release` (stores): synchronizes-with the paired op (one-flag-protects-data patterns). `AcqRel`: read-modify-write that does both. `SeqCst`: total global order across all SeqCst ops — strongest, slowest, expensive on weak-memory architectures.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: `SeqCst` is the safest default but rarely the right one. Identify what synchronizes-with what: `Relaxed` for counters with no read-after-write across threads; `Acquire`/`Release` pairs for locks, channels, and one-shot signalling; `AcqRel` for fetch-update RMW; `SeqCst` only when you actually need a total global order (rare).

source: Rust for Rustaceans, ch. 9 §Allocation; Rust Performance Book §Heap Allocations
rule_summary: `Box<T>` is for trait objects, recursive types, or genuinely large values you need to move cheaply. Don't wrap small `Copy` or `Clone` types in `Box` for "indirection" — heap allocation costs an alloc/dealloc plus a pointer chase on every access. Stack values are faster, simpler, and the compiler optimizes them better. `Box<String>` and `Box<Vec<T>>` are double indirection through a type that already owns a heap allocation.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: Reach for `Box<T>` only when one of three reasons applies — `Box<dyn Trait>` for trait-object polymorphism, `Box<Self>` for recursive types, or `Box<T>` for genuinely large `T` you want to move cheaply. For everything else, store `T` directly. `Box<u32>`, `Box<bool>`, and `Box<String>` are usually a code-smell.

source: Rust Reference §Unsafety; Rustonomicon §Working with Unsafe; clippy::undocumented_unsafe_blocks
rule_summary: Every `unsafe { ... }` block must be paired with a `// SAFETY: <which invariants this caller satisfies>` comment immediately above it. The comment names the obligations from the called API's `# Safety` section and explains why this caller meets them. The convention is enforced project-wide by `clippy::undocumented_unsafe_blocks`. A bare `unsafe { ... }` is a code review red flag — it asserts soundness without justifying it.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: Never write a bare `unsafe { ... }` block. Pair each one with a `// SAFETY: ...` comment that names the specific invariants you are asserting and why they hold here (e.g., `the slice is non-empty by the early return above`, `the pointer comes from Box::into_raw and was never reborrowed`). The comment is the audit trail; it is what makes the unsafe block reviewable.

source: Rust Reference §unsafe-fn; Rustonomicon §Safe and Unsafe APIs; clippy::missing_safety_doc
rule_summary: Declaring `unsafe fn foo()` says "calling this requires invariants the type system cannot check." Document those preconditions in a `# Safety` rustdoc section above the function; callers must, in their own `// SAFETY:` comment at each call site, explain how *they* satisfy each precondition (substituting concrete justifications, not just restating the contract). Don't mark a function `unsafe` defensively — only mark it when a caller can break memory safety by misusing it. `clippy::missing_safety_doc` flags `pub unsafe fn` without `# Safety` docs.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: When you write `unsafe fn`, write a `/// # Safety` rustdoc section listing every precondition a caller must uphold (e.g., "`ptr` must be non-null and valid for `len * size_of::<T>()` bytes"). If you cannot enumerate the preconditions, the function should not be `unsafe` — it is either safe (reword the API) or unsoundly designed (reshape the API).

source: Rust for Rustaceans, ch. 9 §Working with Unsafe; Rustonomicon §Working with Unsafe
rule_summary: Wrap only the operation that genuinely requires `unsafe`, not the surrounding logic. A 1- or 2-line `unsafe { ... }` block isolates exactly the operation under review; a multi-line block hides which operations are actually unsafe inside otherwise-safe code. Bounds checks, pointer construction, and length computation should live outside the unsafe block whenever possible.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: When you reach for `unsafe`, scope it to the exact unsafe operation. Compute pointers, lengths, and bounds checks in safe code; drop into `unsafe { ... }` only for the dereference, the `from_raw_parts`, the `assume_init`, etc. The reviewer should be able to read the unsafe block in isolation and see the single operation it covers.

source: std::mem::MaybeUninit; Rustonomicon §Working with Uninitialized Memory; std::ptr::addr_of_mut!
rule_summary: `mem::uninitialized<T>()` is undefined behavior for almost every `T` and is deprecated; reach for `MaybeUninit<T>` instead. Pattern: `let mut x = MaybeUninit::<T>::uninit();` then write each field through `addr_of_mut!((*x.as_mut_ptr()).field).write(value)` (or, on Rust 1.82+, the equivalent `&raw mut (*x.as_mut_ptr()).field`); never go through a `&mut T` to the partially-initialized value. Call `assume_init()` only once *every* field is initialized. Critical: constructing a `&mut T` to a `MaybeUninit<T>` is itself undefined behavior — the `&mut` is invalid the moment it exists, regardless of whether you read from it.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: For delayed initialization use `MaybeUninit<T>`, not `mem::uninitialized` (deprecated, immediate UB for non-zero-sized non-Copy types). Write fields through raw pointers via `addr_of_mut!` (or `&raw mut`), never through a `&mut T` to the partially-initialized value. Call `assume_init()` only after every field has been written.

source: std::cell::UnsafeCell; Rust Reference §Interior Mutability; Rustonomicon §Aliasing
rule_summary: `UnsafeCell<T>` is the *only* type the language permits to obtain a `*mut T` from a shared reference (`&UnsafeCell<T>` → `.get()` → `*mut T`). Every safe interior-mutability type (`Cell`, `RefCell`, `Mutex`, `RwLock`, atomics, `OnceCell`) is built on top of it. Casting `&T` to `&mut T` through any other path is undefined behavior even if no one is currently reading the `&T`. Don't roll a custom `Cell` with raw pointer casts; use the existing wrappers, or build directly on `UnsafeCell` with documented invariants.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: For shared-reference mutation, pick the safest existing wrapper (`Cell` for Copy, `RefCell` for !Copy single-threaded, `Mutex`/`RwLock` for cross-thread, atomics for primitives). If none fit, build on `UnsafeCell<T>` with explicit safety documentation. Never cast `*const T` to `*mut T` to mutate behind a shared reference unless `T` is inside an `UnsafeCell` — that's UB regardless of whether anyone observes the mutation.

source: Rustonomicon §Aliasing; Rustonomicon §References; Stacked Borrows model (Rust unsafe code guidelines)
rule_summary: Rust's reference rules apply *everywhere a reference is live*, not just where the borrow checker can see them: a `&mut T` is unique (no other reference to the same location), a `&T` is shared (any number of `&T`, but no `&mut T` to the same location). Raw pointers do not impose aliasing rules, but the moment you materialize a `&mut T` from a raw pointer, every other live reference to that location becomes invalid under the Stacked Borrows model. Keep raw pointers raw until the last moment; convert to `&T`/`&mut T` only when you can prove no other reference is live.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: Treat references as exclusive (`&mut`) or shared (`&`) globally, not lexically. When you cross between safe and unsafe code with raw pointers, do not materialize a `&mut T` from a raw pointer if any other reference to the same allocation is live — even a `&T`, even one that was created earlier and is about to be dropped. The Stacked Borrows model says the `&mut` invalidates the earlier reference at the moment the `&mut` is created.

source: Rust Reference §Foreign Function Interface; Rust for Rustaceans, ch. 11 §FFI; std::panic::catch_unwind; RFC 2945 (extern "C-unwind")
rule_summary: Three rules at the `extern "C"` boundary: (1) Rust panics that unwind into foreign code are undefined behavior under the default `extern "C"` ABI — wrap every `extern "C" fn` body in `std::panic::catch_unwind` and convert `Err` into an error code or sentinel value. The opt-in `extern "C-unwind"` ABI (stabilized 1.71) explicitly permits unwinding for callers that want it. (2) Never expose Rust references (`&T`/`&mut T`) across the boundary — C cannot uphold validity invariants. Use `*const T`/`*mut T` and document the lifetime/ownership rules. (3) `extern "C"` functions cannot be generic; instantiate concrete wrappers per type.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: Every `extern "C" fn` body wraps its work in `std::panic::catch_unwind` and converts panics into a sentinel error value. Function signatures use `*const T`/`*mut T`, never `&T`/`&mut T`, and the rustdoc above the function documents who allocates, who frees, and what null/length conventions hold. No generic `extern "C"` functions — write concrete `extern "C" fn parse_u32_buffer(...)` wrappers around generic helpers.

source: Rust Reference §Type Layout (repr(C), repr(<int>)); std::ptr::NonNull; std::ffi::{c_int, c_uchar, c_char}
rule_summary: Types crossing the FFI boundary need a stable layout. `#[repr(C)]` on structs and unions matches the C ABI; `#[repr(<int>)]` (e.g. `#[repr(u8)]`) gives enums a stable discriminant layout; default `repr(Rust)` is *not* stable and must not cross. For nullable pointers use `Option<NonNull<T>>` — niche optimization makes it the same size as `*mut T` and uses the null bit pattern as `None`. Use the concrete ABI types from `std::ffi` (`c_int`, `c_uchar`, `c_char`) rather than Rust's `i32`/`u8`/`i8` to make the C-side mapping explicit.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: Every type that appears in an `extern "C"` signature or as a field of one must have an FFI-safe layout. Annotate structs and unions with `#[repr(C)]`; annotate enums with `#[repr(u8)]` (or another integer); use `Option<NonNull<T>>` instead of `*mut T` when null is meaningful (you get the null check for free in pattern matches); use `c_int`/`c_char`/`c_uchar` for primitives so the C header generator and the reader both see the intended ABI mapping.

source: std::ffi::{CStr, CString}; Rust Reference §Foreign Function Interface; Rust for Rustaceans, ch. 11 §Strings
rule_summary: Strings cross the FFI boundary as `*const c_char` (or `*mut`) with documented ownership: the type does not encode who frees, so the rustdoc must. `CStr::from_ptr(p)` borrows for the duration of `'a` (caller retains ownership); `CString::into_raw()` transfers ownership *out* of Rust and a matching `CString::from_raw()` reclaims it back. Buffers (non-string) cross as `(ptr: *const T, len: usize)` pairs — never raw pointer alone, because length is not recoverable on the C side.
prompt_injection: MANDATORY RULE: Every C string parameter has its ownership documented in the rustdoc: "caller retains and frees" (use `CStr::from_ptr` for a borrow), or "ownership transferred to Rust, will be freed by Rust" (use `CString::from_raw`), or "Rust transfers ownership; C must call <free_fn>" (return `CString::into_raw`). Buffers are always `(ptr, len)` pairs; never expose a length-less raw pointer to a buffer.
